title: Data Cleaning: Real Data Is Never This Clean
learningObjectives: Detect and handle missing values deliberately (drop, fill, or flag) rather than ignoring them
Detect and remove duplicate rows introduced by a merge or a re-ingested API response
Identify outliers that are likely sensor errors rather than genuine extreme readings, and decide what to do with them
Justify a cleaning decision with a specific reason rather than applying a default blindly
whyItMatters: The unified feature table you just built almost certainly has gaps, duplicates, and a few physically implausible values (a negative demand reading, a 90°C temperature) — and every one of those, left unaddressed, quietly degrades every forecasting model trained on top of it in Mission 3.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's data-quality gate — the step that stands between raw merged data and anything a model is allowed to train on, catching exactly the kind of corrupted input that produces confidently wrong forecasts.

problem.summary: The merged table has gaps, duplicates, and an implausible reading.
problem.content: Your unified feature table has some `NaN` values where the merge didn't find a match, a few duplicate rows from a re-run ingestion job, and at least one demand reading of -400 kW that's clearly a sensor error, not a real measurement.

motivation.summary: A model trained on dirty data fails silently, producing confidently wrong forecasts.
motivation.content: A forecasting model trained on dirty data doesn't fail loudly — it just learns the wrong patterns and produces confidently wrong forecasts, which is worse than an obvious crash because nobody notices until real decisions are already being made on bad predictions.

theory.summary: Handle missingness deliberately, remove duplicates after inspection, and catch outliers with domain knowledge first.
theory.content: `df.isna().sum()` counts missing values per column, the first step before deciding what to do about them: `df.dropna()` removes rows with any missing value (only appropriate when missingness is rare and dropping won't bias the data), `df.fillna(...)` fills gaps (forward-fill for slowly-changing values like price, a computed mean for noisy sensor gaps), or leaving a flag column marking rows as imputed so a model can account for the uncertainty later. `df.duplicated()` flags duplicate rows so they can be inspected before removal with `df.drop_duplicates()`. Outliers are best identified with domain knowledge first (a demand reading can't be negative) and statistical methods second (values many standard deviations from the mean) — domain knowledge alone would catch the -400 kW reading; statistics alone might not flag it if it happens to fall within a plausible-looking range for a different sensor.

visualExplanation.summary: Missing-value gaps, duplicate rows, and an outlier are each addressed by a distinct fix.
visualExplanation.content: The unified feature table shows missing-value gaps, duplicate row highlights, and an outlier reading flagged in red, each addressed by a distinct visual 'fix' operation (fill, drop, cap/flag) side by side.

interactiveDemo.summary: Apply cleaning operations to a live messy table and watch problems get fixed.
interactiveDemo.content: Apply `dropna`/`fillna`/`drop_duplicates` operations to a live messy sample table and watch the row count and highlighted problem cells update after each operation.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: quantify missingness, fill deliberately, dedupe, and filter an outlier.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
print(combined.isna().sum())

combined["price_per_kwh"] = combined["price_per_kwh"].ffill()
combined = combined.drop_duplicates(subset=["timestamp", "source"])

combined = combined[combined["demand_kw"] >= 0]
```

independentCoding.summary: Apply and justify a full cleaning pass on a provided messy table.
independentCoding.content: Given a provided messy feature table with missing weather values, duplicate rows, and at least one implausible reading, apply and justify a cleaning strategy for each problem you find.

debugging.summary: Fix an overly broad dropna() call that deletes far more rows than necessary.
debugging.content: Cleaning code calls `.dropna()` on the entire table as a first step, silently deleting far more rows than necessary because one rarely-populated column had lots of missing values. Identify the overly broad drop and replace it with a more targeted fix.

reflection.summary: An unnoticed extreme outlier can cause real downstream harm.
reflection.content: What real, later damage could an unnoticed -400 kW reading do if it made it into a model's training data unaddressed?

teachBack.summary: Explain why blanket dropna() is usually the wrong default.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone why 'just drop every row with any missing value' is usually the wrong default cleaning strategy, using the overly broad `dropna()` bug as your example.

miniProject.summary: Run a full, documented data-quality pass on the feature table.
miniProject.content: Run a full data-quality pass on the Digital Twin's unified feature table: quantify missingness, remove duplicates, flag and handle at least one outlier, and document every decision with a specific reason.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the data-quality gate every future model training run depends on.
portfolioEvidence.content: This cleaned feature table is what actually gets handed to Mission 3's forecasting models — the data-quality gate every future model training run depends on having passed.

labs.title: Quality Pass
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise on a deliberately messy feature table requiring justified handling of missing values, duplicates, and at least one physically implausible outlier.

simulations.title: Data Quality Fix Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows a messy table's missing values, duplicates, and outliers each resolved by a distinct fix operation, tracked live.

flashcards.front: Why check domain knowledge (e.g. 'demand can't be negative') before relying purely on statistical outlier detection?
flashcards.back: A statistically 'normal-looking' value can still be physically impossible, and a genuinely extreme but real value might get flagged by statistics alone. Domain knowledge catches errors that pure statistics would miss or wrongly flag.

flashcards.front: Why is calling df.dropna() on the whole table often the wrong first move?
flashcards.back: If even one column is sparsely populated, a blanket dropna() removes every row missing any value in any column, potentially discarding far more good data than necessary — a targeted, column-specific strategy is usually better.

rubric: Each cleaning decision (fill, drop, flag) is justified with a specific reason, not applied as an unexamined default
Correctly identifies at least one outlier using domain knowledge, not just statistical thresholds
Avoids overly broad dropna()/drop_duplicates() calls that discard more data than necessary

references.title: pandas User Guide — Working with missing data
